solution: When a power is odd, peel off one factor and substitute (\( u = \cos x \) if \( \sin \) is odd, \( u = \sin x \) if \( \cos \) is odd); when both are even, use half-angle identities. Over \( [0, \pi/2] \) this gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct value, e.g. from mishandling the substitution's factor.
This halves the correct value.
This triples the correct value through an arithmetic slip.
This scales the correct value by 1/3.
This scales the correct value by 3/2.
This scales the correct value by 2/3.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi} [[c]]\sin^{[[n]]} x\,dx \) (a pure odd power of sine, no cosine factor).
solution: Since \( [[n]] \) is odd, peel off one factor of \( \sin x \), write the rest as \( (1-\cos^{2}x)^{([[n]]-1)/2} \), and substitute \( u=\cos x \). Carrying this out from \( 0 \) to \( \pi \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct value, as if only integrating over \( [0,\pi/2] \).
This doubles the correct value.
This multiplies \( c \) and \( n \) directly instead of carrying out the substitution.
This divides \( c \) by \( n \) instead of carrying out the substitution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\cos^{[[n]]} x\,dx \) (a pure even power of cosine, no sine factor) using the Wallis formula.
solution: By the Wallis formula, \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{n}x\,dx = \dfrac{(n-1)!!}{n!!}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{2} \) for even \( n \). With \( n=[[n]] \), multiplying by \( [[c]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This uses the \( n=0 \) Wallis value \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \) regardless of the actual power.
This uses the odd-power Wallis pattern instead of the even-power one.
This scales the correct value by 3/2.
This scales the correct value by 2/3.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{2\pi} [[c]]\sin^{2}([[k]]x)\cos^{2}([[k]]x)\,dx \) by first rewriting the integrand with a double-angle identity.
solution: Since \( \sin^{2}([[k]]x)\cos^{2}([[k]]x) = \tfrac{1}{4}\sin^{2}(2[[k]]x) = \tfrac{1}{8}\left(1-\cos(4[[k]]x)\right) \), integrating the constant term \( \tfrac{1}{8} \) over \( [0,2\pi] \) (the cosine term integrates to \( 0 \) over whole periods) gives \( [[c]]\cdot\tfrac{1}{8}\cdot 2\pi = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This uses \( \tfrac{1}{4} \) instead of \( \tfrac{1}{8} \) for the average value of \( \sin^{2}(2kx) \).
This uses \( \tfrac{1}{4} \) as the average value and forgets to halve it again.
This scales the correct value by 3/2.
This scales the correct value by 2/3.
